                            MinDistance,
                            DistAD,
                            DistAD + Abs(
                                Sum(
                                    PointA,
                                    -PicketFrom
                                )
                            ) + Abs(
                                Sum(
                                    PointD,
                                    -PicketTo
                                )
                            ),
                            DistAC,
                            DistAC + Abs(
                                Sum(
                                    PointA,
                                    -PicketFrom
                                )
                            ) + Abs(
                                Sum(
                                    PointC,
                                    -PicketTo
                                )
                            )
                        )
finish for all DistAC,
                    DistAD,
                    DistBC,
                    DistBD

safeHtmlItem.html:
To complete this logic, you need to align each distance from the matrix with its corresponding anchor points. Since the matrix distance connects two specific stations, your offsets (the pickets) must be calculated relative to those exact stations.

Here is the completed Switch logic for all four cases:

Switch(
    MinDistance,
    /* Case 1: Start at A, End at C */
    DistAC, 
    DistAC + Abs(PointA - PicketFrom) + Abs(PointC - PicketTo),

    /* Case 2: Start at A, End at D */
    DistAD, 
    DistAD + Abs(PointA - PicketFrom) + Abs(PointD - PicketTo),

    /* Case 3: Start at B, End at C */
    DistBC, 
    DistBC + Abs(PointB - PicketFrom) + Abs(PointC - PicketTo),

    /* Case 4: Start at B, End at D */
    DistBD, 
    DistBD + Abs(PointB - PicketFrom) + Abs(PointD - PicketTo),

    /* Default Fallback */
    0
)

Why this works:

  • Point Matching: Notice that if the matrix uses DistBC, the start offset is forced to use PointB and the end offset uses PointC. This ensures that even if the user picked the "wrong" station order, the math aligns with the shortest path found in your matrix.
  • Vector Addition: By using Abs(Point - Picket), you are calculating the distance from the station to the work site on that specific segment and adding it to the total "main line" distance.
  • Code Cleanliness: I replaced the Sum(Point, -Picket) with standard Point - Picket syntax; it does the same thing but is much easier to read in Power Apps.

Would you like me to wrap this final Switch back into the full With statement so you can copy the whole block at once?

To finalize your Power Fx logic, we need to wrap the calculation in a Text function for display. I’ve also added a final Round function, as distance calculations involving pickets (divided by 10) can sometimes result in floating-point trailing decimals (e.g., 10.0000000004).

Here is the complete, refactored code ready for your Text property:

Text(
    With(
        {
            /* 1. SETUP INPUTS */
            SFromA: Int(varContractWorkRecrod.StationFrom_UICcode.UIC_code),
            SFromB: Int(varContractWorkRecrod.StationFromB_UICcode.UIC_code),
            SToA:   Int(dd_ContractWorkStationFrom_1.Selected.UIC_code),
            SToB:   Int(dd_ContractWorkStationTo_1.Selected.UIC_code),
            
            PFrom:  If(varContractWorkRecrod.LocationPointFrom > 0, varContractWorkRecrod.LocationPointFrom, Blank()),
            PTo:    If(!IsBlank(objCWPicketPickerParams), Sum(objCWPicketPickerParams.KM, objCWPicketPickerParams.Pkt / 10), Blank())
        },
        With(
            {
                /* 2. GET KM COORDINATES (Bi-directional Lookup) */
                Point_FromA: Coalesce(LookUp(trip_Distances, TaskerStationFrom_UICcode.UIC_code = Text(SFromA) && TaskerStationTo_UICcode.UIC_code = Text(SFromB)).StationFromPoint, LookUp(trip_Distances, TaskerStationFrom_UICcode.UIC_code = Text(SFromB) && TaskerStationTo_UICcode.UIC_code = Text(SFromA)).StationToPoint),
                Point_FromB: Coalesce(LookUp(trip_Distances, TaskerStationFrom_UICcode.UIC_code = Text(SFromB) && TaskerStationTo_UICcode.UIC_code = Text(SFromA)).StationFromPoint, LookUp(trip_Distances, TaskerStationFrom_UICcode.UIC_code = Text(SFromA) && TaskerStationTo_UICcode.UIC_code = Text(SFromB)).StationToPoint),
                Point_ToA:   Coalesce(LookUp(trip_Distances, TaskerStationFrom_UICcode.UIC_code = Text(SToA) && TaskerStationTo_UICcode.UIC_code = Text(SToB)).StationFromPoint, LookUp(trip_Distances, TaskerStationFrom_UICcode.UIC_code = Text(SToB) && TaskerStationTo_UICcode.UIC_code = Text(SToA)).StationToPoint),
                Point_ToB:   Coalesce(LookUp(trip_Distances, TaskerStationFrom_UICcode.UIC_code = Text(SToB) && TaskerStationTo_UICcode.UIC_code = Text(SToA)).StationFromPoint, LookUp(trip_Distances, TaskerStationFrom_UICcode.UIC_code = Text(SToA) && TaskerStationTo_UICcode.UIC_code = Text(SToB)).StationToPoint),

                /* 3. FETCH ALL 4 POSSIBLE DISTANCES (Handles Matrix with no Blanks) */
                D_AC: Coalesce(LookUp(trip_DistanceMatrices, StationA = SFromA && StationB = SToA).Distance, 999999),
                D_AD: Coalesce(LookUp(trip_DistanceMatrices, StationA = SFromA && StationB = SToB).Distance, 999999),
                D_BC: Coalesce(LookUp(trip_DistanceMatrices, StationA = SFromB && StationB = SToA).Distance, 999999),
                D_BD: Coalesce(LookUp(trip_DistanceMatrices, StationA = SFromB && StationB = SToB).Distance, 999999)
            },
            With(
                {
                    /* 4. FIND THE SHORTEST PATH (Determines true entry/exit points) */
                    MinDist: Min(D_AC, D_AD, D_BC, D_BD)
                },
                Round(
                    If(
                        /* CASE: Local Trip (Same segment) */
                        SFromA = SToA && SFromB = SToB,
                        Abs(Coalesce(PFrom, Point_FromA) - Coalesce(PTo, Point_FromA)),

                        /* CASE: Across Segments (Replaces hardcoded 50) */
                        Switch(MinDist,
                            D_AC, D_AC - Abs(Point_FromA - Coalesce(PFrom, Point_FromA)) + Abs(Point_ToA - Coalesce(PTo, Point_ToA)),
                            D_BC, D_BC - Abs(Point_FromB - Coalesce(PFrom, Point_FromB)) + Abs(Point_ToA - Coalesce(PTo, Point_ToA)),
                            D_AD, D_AD - Abs(Point_FromA - Coalesce(PFrom, Point_FromA)) + Abs(Point_ToB - Coalesce(PTo, Point_ToB)),
                            D_BD, D_BD - Abs(Point_FromB - Coalesce(PFrom, Point_FromB)) + Abs(Point_ToB - Coalesce(PTo, Point_ToB)),
                            0
                        )
                    ),
                    2 /* Rounding to 2 decimal places */
                )
            )
        )
    ),
    "##0.00" & " km"
)

Why this works:

  • Logical Anchor: By using the MinDist, the code automatically identifies which pair of stations represents the most efficient route. It then applies your intermediate "pickets" as offsets from those specific stations.
  • Scannability: The Switch statement replaces the complex If nesting, making it much easier for you to debug if the database values change.
  • The "50" Solution: It calculates the distance dynamically by taking the full station-to-station distance, subtracting the "unused" part of the first segment, and adding the "used" part of the final segment.

Would you like me to show you how to add an error message if MinDist returns 999999 (meaning no route was found in the matrix)?

title: Prompted you cant get blank values from Distance matrixe so it might not work as indented

safeHtmlItem.html:
Ah, that changes things! If your trip_DistanceMatrices always returns a value (even if it's a huge number or zero for invalid routes) rather than a blank, we can't use IsBlank to decide the route.

To handle this, we need to compare the distances and pick the minimum. If the user picks the wrong station order (e.g., they picked the "A" side of a segment when they are actually traveling from the "B" side), the distance to the destination will be slightly longer or shorter.

Here is the refactored version that solves the "50" hardcode by using the Minimum Distance logic to auto-determine the correct entry/exit points.

Refactored Logic (Min-Distance Approach)

With(
    {
        /* 1. SETUP INPUTS */
        SFromA: varContractWorkRecrod.StationFrom_UICcode.UIC_code,
        SFromB: varContractWorkRecrod.StationFromB_UICcode.UIC_code,
        SToA:   dd_ContractWorkStationFrom_1.Selected.UIC_code,
        SToB:   dd_ContractWorkStationTo_1.Selected.UIC_code,
        
        PFrom:  If(varContractWorkRecrod.LocationPointFrom > 0, varContractWorkRecrod.LocationPointFrom, Blank()),
        PTo:    If(!IsBlank(objCWPicketPickerParams), Sum(objCWPicketPickerParams.KM, objCWPicketPickerParams.Pkt / 10), Blank())
    },
    With(
        {
            /* 2. GET KM COORDINATES (Still using Coalesce for the 1-way DB) */
            Point_FromA: Coalesce(LookUp(trip_Distances, TaskerStationFrom_UICcode.UIC_code = SFromA && TaskerStationTo_UICcode.UIC_code = SFromB).StationFromPoint, LookUp(trip_Distances, TaskerStationFrom_UICcode.UIC_code = SFromB && TaskerStationTo_UICcode.UIC_code = SFromA).StationToPoint),
            Point_FromB: Coalesce(LookUp(trip_Distances, TaskerStationFrom_UICcode.UIC_code = SFromB && TaskerStationTo_UICcode.UIC_code = SFromA).StationFromPoint, LookUp(trip_Distances, TaskerStationFrom_UICcode.UIC_code = SFromA && TaskerStationTo_UICcode.UIC_code = SFromB).StationToPoint),
            Point_ToA:   Coalesce(LookUp(trip_Distances, TaskerStationFrom_UICcode.UIC_code = SToA && TaskerStationTo_UICcode.UIC_code = SToB).StationFromPoint, LookUp(trip_Distances, TaskerStationFrom_UICcode.UIC_code = SToB && TaskerStationTo_UICcode.UIC_code = SToA).StationToPoint),
            Point_ToB:   Coalesce(LookUp(trip_Distances, TaskerStationFrom_UICcode.UIC_code = SToB && TaskerStationTo_UICcode.UIC_code = SToA).StationFromPoint, LookUp(trip_Distances, TaskerStationFrom_UICcode.UIC_code = SToA && TaskerStationTo_UICcode.UIC_code = SToB).StationToPoint),

            /* 3. FETCH ALL 4 POSSIBLE DISTANCES */
            D_AC: Coalesce(LookUp(trip_DistanceMatrices, StationA = Value(SFromA) && StationB = Value(SToA)).Distance, 999999),
            D_AD: Coalesce(LookUp(trip_DistanceMatrices, StationA = Value(SFromA) && StationB = Value(SToB)).Distance, 999999),
            D_BC: Coalesce(LookUp(trip_DistanceMatrices, StationA = Value(SFromB) && StationB = Value(SToA)).Distance, 999999),
            D_BD: Coalesce(LookUp(trip_DistanceMatrices, StationA = Value(SFromB) && StationB = Value(SToB)).Distance, 999999)
        },
        With(
            {
                /* 4. FIND THE SHORTEST PATH (This handles the "Wrong Order" problem) */
                MinDist: Min(D_AC, D_AD, D_BC, D_BD)
            },
            If(
                /* CASE: Local Trip (Same segment) */
                SFromA = SToA && SFromB = SToB,
                Abs(Coalesce(PFrom, Point_FromA) - Coalesce(PTo, Point_FromA)),

                /* CASE: Across Segments (Replaces the Hardcoded 50) */
                /* We calculate the distance based on which Matrix combo was the shortest */
                Switch(MinDist,
                    D_AC, D_AC - Abs(Point_FromA - Coalesce(PFrom, Point_FromA)) + Abs(Point_ToA - Coalesce(PTo, Point_ToA)),
                    D_BC, D_BC - Abs(Point_FromB - Coalesce(PFrom, Point_FromB)) + Abs(Point_ToA - Coalesce(PTo, Point_ToA)),
                    D_AD, D_AD - Abs(Point_FromA - Coalesce(PFrom, Point_FromA)) + Abs(Point_ToB - Coalesce(PTo, Point_ToB)),
                    D_BD, D_BD - Abs(Point_FromB - Coalesce(PFrom, Point_FromB)) + Abs(Point_ToB - Coalesce(PTo, Point_ToB)),
                    0
                )
            )
        )
    )
)

Why this works better

  • The Matrix Problem: By assigning 999999 to any LookUp that fails and then using Min(), we force the app to ignore invalid routes and focus only on the shortest path between the two station-areas.
  • Vector Math: This formula Distance - StartOffset + EndOffset is the standard way to calculate linear distances.
    • Distance: Length between two "anchors" (Station A and Station C).
    • StartOffset: If your PicketFrom is 2km away from Station A, we subtract that 2km because you started "late."
    • EndOffset: If your PicketTo is 3km away from Station C, we add that 3km because you traveled "further" into the next segment.

Refactoring Notes

  1. Switch vs Nested If: Using Switch(MinDist...) is much easier to read than 5 layers of If statements.
  2. Point Normalization: The Point_FromA (and others) logic ensures that even if your trip_Distances table only has A -> B, you can still find the KM value if the user inputs B -> A.

Would you like me to add a validation check to see if the calculated distance is negative (which might happen if the pickets are placed outside the expected segment)?

You are dealing with a classic Vector Problem. When you have intermediate points (pickets) and potentially "flipped" station inputs (A-B vs B-A), you cannot rely on simple addition.

Here is the refactored code. I have removed the nested If spaghetti and replaced the hardcoded 50 with a logic that:

  1. Normalizes Inputs: Uses Coalesce to handle your one-way database (finding the point regardless of A->B or B->A order).
  2. Auto-Corrects Direction: It calculates the distance for all 4 possible combinations of your input stations (A-C, A-D, B-C, B-D) and picks the one that makes sense (the shortest valid route).
  3. Solves the Picket Offset: It calculates the trip as: Base Distance - (Distance traveled at Start) + (Distance traveled at End).

Refactored Power FX

With(
    {
        /* 1. INITIALIZE & CLEAN INPUTS */
        SFromA: varContractWorkRecrod.StationFrom_UICcode.UIC_code,
        SFromB: varContractWorkRecrod.StationFromB_UICcode.UIC_code,
        SToA:   dd_ContractWorkStationFrom_1.Selected.UIC_code,
        SToB:   dd_ContractWorkStationTo_1.Selected.UIC_code,
        
        /* Pickets: If blank, we treat them as 0 for calculation safety */
        PFrom:  If(varContractWorkRecrod.LocationPointFrom > 0, varContractWorkRecrod.LocationPointFrom, Blank()),
        PTo:    If(!IsBlank(objCWPicketPickerParams), Sum(objCWPicketPickerParams.KM, objCWPicketPickerParams.Pkt / 10), Blank())
    },
    With(
        {
            /* 2. GET COORDINATES (Handle One-Way DB Logic) 
               We try A->B, if blank we try B->A to find the KM point */
            Point_FromA: Coalesce(
                LookUp(trip_Distances, TaskerStationFrom_UICcode.UIC_code = SFromA && TaskerStationTo_UICcode.UIC_code = SFromB).StationFromPoint,
                LookUp(trip_Distances, TaskerStationFrom_UICcode.UIC_code = SFromB && TaskerStationTo_UICcode.UIC_code = SFromA).StationToPoint
            ),
            Point_FromB: Coalesce(
                LookUp(trip_Distances, TaskerStationFrom_UICcode.UIC_code = SFromB && TaskerStationTo_UICcode.UIC_code = SFromA).StationFromPoint,
                LookUp(trip_Distances, TaskerStationFrom_UICcode.UIC_code = SFromA && TaskerStationTo_UICcode.UIC_code = SFromB).StationToPoint
            ),
            Point_ToA: Coalesce(
                LookUp(trip_Distances, TaskerStationFrom_UICcode.UIC_code = SToA && TaskerStationTo_UICcode.UIC_code = SToB).StationFromPoint,
                LookUp(trip_Distances, TaskerStationFrom_UICcode.UIC_code = SToB && TaskerStationTo_UICcode.UIC_code = SToA).StationToPoint
            ),
            Point_ToB: Coalesce(
                LookUp(trip_Distances, TaskerStationFrom_UICcode.UIC_code = SToB && TaskerStationTo_UICcode.UIC_code = SToA).StationFromPoint,
                LookUp(trip_Distances, TaskerStationFrom_UICcode.UIC_code = SToA && TaskerStationTo_UICcode.UIC_code = SToB).StationToPoint
            ),

            /* 3. GET BASE DISTANCES (The 4 possibilities) */
            Dist_AC: LookUp(trip_DistanceMatrices, StationA = Value(SFromA) && StationB = Value(SToA)).Distance,
            Dist_AD: LookUp(trip_DistanceMatrices, StationA = Value(SFromA) && StationB = Value(SToB)).Distance,
            Dist_BC: LookUp(trip_DistanceMatrices, StationA = Value(SFromB) && StationB = Value(SToA)).Distance,
            Dist_BD: LookUp(trip_DistanceMatrices, StationA = Value(SFromB) && StationB = Value(SToB)).Distance
        },
        
        /* 4. DETERMINE BEST ROUTE (Fixes Wrong Input Order) */
        With({
            BestRoute: 
                If(
                    // If Start/End stations are same, it's a local trip
                    SFromA = SToA, "Local",
                    // Otherwise, find the shortest non-blank path to determine 'Real' Start/End
                    !IsBlank(Dist_AC) && (IsBlank(Dist_BC) || Dist_AC <= Dist_BC), "AC",
                    !IsBlank(Dist_BC), "BC",
                    !IsBlank(Dist_AD), "AD",
                    "BD" 
                )
        },
            /* 5. FINAL CALCULATION (Fixes the Hardcoded 50) */
            Switch(BestRoute,
                "Local", 
                    Abs(Coalesce(PFrom, Point_FromA) - Coalesce(PTo, Point_FromA)),
                
                "AC",
                    Dist_AC 
                    - Abs(Point_FromA - Coalesce(PFrom, Point_FromA)) 
                    + Abs(Point_ToA - Coalesce(PTo, Point_ToA)),

                "BC",
                    Dist_BC 
                    - Abs(Point_FromB - Coalesce(PFrom, Point_FromB)) 
                    + Abs(Point_ToA - Coalesce(PTo, Point_ToA)),

                "AD",
                    Dist_AD 
                    - Abs(Point_FromA - Coalesce(PFrom, Point_FromA)) 
                    + Abs(Point_ToB - Coalesce(PTo, Point_ToB)),

                "BD",
                    Dist_BD 
                    - Abs(Point_FromB - Coalesce(PFrom, Point_FromB)) 
                    + Abs(Point_ToB - Coalesce(PTo, Point_ToB)),
                
                // Fallback (Should not happen if DB is complete)
                0 
            )
        )
    )
)

How this solves your problems

  1. The "50" Hardcode:
    • Previously, you checked If(!IsBlank(PicketFrom) && !IsBlank(PicketTo), 50...).
    • Now, look at the "AC" case in the Switch. It calculates: BaseDistance - StartOffset + EndOffset. This works mathematically whether you have pickets or not (because of the Coalesce to the station point).
  2. The Wrong Input Order:
    • The BestRoute block checks Dist_AC, Dist_BC, etc.
    • If the user selected Station A, but the actual track connection starts from Station B (the neighbor), Dist_BC will likely be the valid distance found in your matrix, and Dist_AC will be blank or larger. The Switch then automatically uses the coordinates for B to calculate the correct distance.
  3. Calculations:
    • Start Offset: Abs(StationPoint - PicketPoint). We subtract this because if you start after the station (at a picket), you have traveled less than the full station-to-station distance.
    • End Offset: We add this because you are traveling into the destination segment.

Next Step

Would you like me to explain how to add a visual indicator (like a label color change) to warn the user if the app detected they picked the "Wrong" start station (e.g., if it switched to using Dist_BC instead of Dist_AC)?

safeHtmlItem.html:
Calculating distances between intermediate points (pickets) on a railway or transit network is a classic logic puzzle because of how the data is structured.

The Problem with the "50" Case

The hardcoded 50 in your code occurs when both a PicketFrom and a PicketTo are present, and the trip spans between two different station segments.

To solve this, you need to find the "anchor" distance between the two main stations and then add or subtract the relative offsets of your intermediate points based on which direction you are traveling.

Refactored Power FX Logic

Here is a cleaner approach. Instead of deeply nested If statements, we calculate the possible segment distances and then apply the picket offsets.

With(
    {
        /* 1. INITIALIZE INPUTS */
        SFromA: Int(varContractWorkRecrod.StationFrom_UICcode.UIC_code),
        SFromB: Int(varContractWorkRecrod.StationFromB_UICcode.UIC_code),
        SToA:   Int(dd_ContractWorkStationFrom_1.Selected.UIC_code),
        SToB:   Int(dd_ContractWorkStationTo_1.Selected.UIC_code),
        
        PFrom:  Coalesce(varContractWorkRecrod.LocationPointFrom, 0),
        PTo:    If(!IsBlank(objCWPicketPickerParams), 
                    objCWPicketPickerParams.KM + (objCWPicketPickerParams.Pkt / 10), 0)
    },
    With(
        {
            /* 2. LOOKUP DISTANCES (Handling A->B vs B->A) */
            // Using a helper to find the 'real' station start/end points for the segments
            BaseDist: Coalesce(
                LookUp(trip_DistanceMatrices, StationA = SFromA && StationB = SToA).Distance,
                0
            ),
            // Offset logic: Find where the picket starts relative to Station A
            StartPointRef: Coalesce(
                LookUp(trip_Distances, TaskerStationFrom_UICcode.UIC_code = Text(SFromA) && TaskerStationTo_UICcode.UIC_code = Text(SFromB)).StationFromPoint,
                LookUp(trip_Distances, TaskerStationFrom_UICcode.UIC_code = Text(SFromB) && TaskerStationTo_UICcode.UIC_code = Text(SFromA)).StationToPoint
            ),
            EndPointRef: Coalesce(
                LookUp(trip_Distances, TaskerStationFrom_UICcode.UIC_code = Text(SToA) && TaskerStationTo_UICcode.UIC_code = Text(SToB)).StationFromPoint,
                LookUp(trip_Distances, TaskerStationFrom_UICcode.UIC_code = Text(SToB) && TaskerStationTo_UICcode.UIC_code = Text(SToA)).StationToPoint
            )
        },
        /* 3. CALCULATION LOGIC */
        If(
            // CASE: Same Segment (Travel between A and B)
            SFromA = SToA && SFromB = SToB,
            Abs(PFrom - PTo),

            // CASE: General Trip (Picket to Picket across stations)
            // Distance = (Distance between Station A and Station C) 
            //            - (Distance from Station A to PicketFrom)
            //            + (Distance from Station C to PicketTo)
            Abs(
                BaseDist 
                - Abs(PFrom - StartPointRef) 
                + Abs(PTo - EndPointRef)
            )
        )
    )
)

Key Refactor Improvements

  1. Coalesce over LookUp: I consolidated the PointAB_A / PointBA_A logic. Since your DB only has one-way entries, the Coalesce checks for A->B and if that's blank, it checks B->A. This removes 2 unnecessary LookUps.
  2. The "50" Fix: The formula BaseDist - Abs(PFrom - StartRef) + Abs(PTo - EndRef) treats the trip as a vector.
    • It starts at the main "From" station.
    • It subtracts the distance already "passed" by the starting picket.
    • It adds the distance "covered" into the destination segment.
  3. Simplified Picket Logic: I used Coalesce(..., 0) for pickets. If a picket isn't provided, it assumes the distance starts/ends exactly at the station (Point 0), making the logic work for both intermediate and station-to-station trips without extra If blocks.

Logic Visualized

Next Step

Would you like me to add a check for cases where the trip might be "backwards" (where the UIC code of Station B is actually closer to the destination than Station A)?

title: Prompted i have a but in powerapps, cant load detailedlist of creatorkit component, but just for some users only write a text for bug ticket in github

safeHtmlItem.html:
Subject: CreatorKit DetailedList Component - Error Loading Control for Specific Users

Description:

The CreatorKit DetailedList component is failing to load for a subset of users within our Power Apps application. While the component functions correctly for most users, a specific group is encountering an "Error loading control" message.

Steps to Reproduce:

  1. Log in to the Power Apps application with an affected user account.
  2. Navigate to the screen containing the DetailedList component.
  3. Observe the "Error loading control" message where the DetailedList should be rendered.

Expected Behavior:

The DetailedList component should load and display data as expected for all users.

Actual Behavior:

The DetailedList component fails to load and displays an "Error loading control" message for a specific group of users.

Environment:

  • Power Apps application name: [Your App Name]
  • Screen Name: [Screen Name where the component is located]
  • CreatorKit Version: [Specify CreatorKit Version if known]
  • Browser: [Specify browser and version if applicable]
  • Operating System: [Specify OS if applicable]

Additional Information:

  • Please investigate why the DetailedList component is failing to load for specific users.
  • The issue does not appear to be related to data access as other components on the same screen load correctly.
  • We have verified that the affected users have the necessary permissions within the application.
  • This issue is impacting [Specify impact, e.g., a critical workflow, a small group of users, etc.].

Note: Please provide any relevant user IDs or account information privately if needed for debugging.

title: Prompted = "{"&"Name:" & I need a " here &  Table_query__3[@StationName]&","& "Code:" &Table_query__3[@Code] &","& "RailType:"&Table_query__3[@RailType]&"},"

safeHtmlItem.html:
="{"&"""Name"":"""&Table_query__3[@StationName]&""","&"""Code"":"""&Table_query__3[@Code]&""","&"""RailType"":"""&Table_query__3[@RailType]&"""},"

Explanation of the changes:

  • Escaping Quotes: To include a literal double quote (") within a text string in Excel (or similar spreadsheet software), you need to "escape" it by using two double quotes ("").
  • Therefore, I added an extra set of double quotes around each key and value to create the correct JSON structure.

title: Prompted make json

safeHtmlItem.html:
Please provide me with the text or data you want me to convert into JSON format. I can't see the image you've sent, so I need the data in a text-based format to work with.

For example, if you want to convert the following data:

Name,Age,City
John,30,New York
Jane,25,London
Peter,35,Paris

I can convert it into this JSON:

[
  {
    "Name": "John",
    "Age": 30,
    "City": "New York"
  },
  {
    "Name": "Jane",
    "Age": 25,
    "City": "London"
  },
  {
    "Name": "Peter",
    "Age": 35,
    "City": "Paris"
  }
]

Just paste the text data here, and I'll do my best to create the JSON you need.

title: Prompted i am creating db table with country info like Lietuva , LT what would be column names

safeHtmlItem.html:
When creating a database table for country information, consider these column names for clarity, consistency, and future flexibility:

Essential Columns:

  • country_id:
    • This should be the primary key, typically an auto-incrementing integer.
    • Purpose: Unique identifier for each country record.
    • Data type: INT (or BIGINT if you anticipate a very large number of countries).
  • country_name:
    • The full, official name of the country (e.g., "Lithuania").
    • Purpose: Human-readable country name.
    • Data type: VARCHAR (or NVARCHAR if you need Unicode support for various character sets).
  • country_code_2:
    • The two-letter ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 country code (e.g., "LT").
    • Purpose: Standardized, short country code.
    • Data type: CHAR(2).
  • country_code_3:
    • The three-letter ISO 3166-1 alpha-3 country code (e.g. "LTU").
    • Purpose: another standardized, short country code.
    • Data type: CHAR(3).

Optional Columns (depending on your needs):

  • country_numeric_code:
    • The three-digit ISO 3166-1 numeric country code.
    • Purpose: Numeric representation of the country.
    • Data type: SMALLINT or INT.
  • continent:
    • The continent the country belongs to.
    • Purpose: Geographical classification.
    • Data type: VARCHAR.
  • region:
    • The region the country belongs to.
    • Purpose: More specific geographical classification.
    • Data type: VARCHAR.
  • subregion:
    • The subregion the country belongs to.
    • Purpose: Even more specific geographical classification.
    • Data type: VARCHAR.
  • capital:
    • The capital city of the country.
    • Purpose: Information about the capital.
    • Data type: VARCHAR.
  • currency_code:
    • The currency code used in the country.
    • Purpose: Information about the currency.
    • Data type: VARCHAR.
  • phone_code:
    • The international phone code used in the country.
    • Purpose: Information about the phone code.
    • Data type: VARCHAR.
  • tld:
    • Top level domain.
    • Purpose: information about the internet TLD.
    • Data type: VARCHAR.
  • is_active:
    • Boolean value that shows if the country is active or not.
    • Purpose: soft delete, or to show if the country is still valid.
    • Data type: Boolean.
  • created_at:
    • Timestamp of when the record was created.
    • Purpose: audit trail.
    • Data type: TIMESTAMP.
  • updated_at:
    • Timestamp of when the record was last updated.
    • Purpose: audit trail.
    • Data type: TIMESTAMP.

Example SQL (PostgreSQL):

CREATE TABLE countries (
    country_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    country_name VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
    country_code_2 CHAR(2) NOT NULL,
    country_code_3 CHAR(3) NOT NULL,
    country_numeric_code SMALLINT,
    continent VARCHAR(50),
    region VARCHAR(100),
    subregion VARCHAR(100),
    capital VARCHAR(100),
    currency_code VARCHAR(3),
    phone_code VARCHAR(10),
    tld VARCHAR(10),
    is_active BOOLEAN DEFAULT TRUE,
    created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    updated_at TIMESTAMP
);

Remember to choose data types and constraints that best fit your specific application's requirements.

title:
Prompted cia yra du atskiri tikslai
Gebėjimas savarankiškai palaikyti UiPath VE ūkį

Ugdyti verslo analitikus, įgyvendinti darbus aktyviai bendradarbiaujant su komandos nariais, deleguojant darbus ir siekiant sėkmingo projekto baigimo.

safeHtmlItem.html:
Taip, teisingai pastebėjote. Šie du punktai iš tiesų yra atskiri tikslai, ir juos reikėtų atskirti, kad būtų aiškiau. Štai patikslintas tikslų sąrašas:

Metiniai Tikslai

  1. SKDV TRD projekto uždarymas:
    • 1.1. Užbaigti visus numatytus darbus (in scope).
  2. Keliolapio kūrimas (Development):
    • 2.1. Parengti sprendimo dizainą (Solution design) ir patvirtinti jį su AT (Architektūros komanda).
    • 2.2. Vykdyti sprendimo kūrimą (Solution development) pagal suplanuotą grafiką.
  3. Incidentų reagavimo laikas:
    • 3.1. Užtikrinti, kad incidentų reagavimo laikas neviršytų 4 valandų.
  4. VE (Verslo Efektyvumo) sprendimų dokumentacija:
    • 4.1. Patvirtinti VE dokumentacijos standartą.
    • 4.2. Užtikrinti, kad visi kuriami sprendimai būtų dokumentuojami pagal patvirtintą standartą.
  5. Gebėjimas savarankiškai palaikyti UiPath VE ūkį:
    • 5.1. Užtikrinti, kad gebėjimai palaikyti UiPath VE ūkį, būtų pilnai savarankiški.
  6. Verslo analitikų ugdymas ir komandinis darbas:
    • 6.1. Ugdyti verslo analitikų kompetencijas.
    • 6.2. Aktyviai bendradarbiauti su komandos nariais, deleguoti užduotis ir siekti sėkmingo projektų užbaigimo.

Papildomi patarimai:

  • Svarbu, kad kiekvienas tikslas būtų aiškiai apibrėžtas ir išmatuojamas.
  • Galite pridėti konkrečius terminus kiekvienam tikslui, kad būtų lengviau stebėti pažangą.
  • Reguliariai peržiūrėkite savo tikslus ir koreguokite veiksmų planą pagal poreikį.
  • SMART tikslų formulavimo metodika visada yra geras sprendimas.

Gebėjimas savarankiškai palaikyti UiPath VE ūkį

Ugdyti verslo analitikus, įgyvendinti darbus aktyviai bendradarbiaujant su komandos nariais, deleguojant darbus ir siekiant sėkmingo projekto baigimo.

perrasyk tvarkingai

safeHtmlItem.html:
Žinoma, štai jūsų metiniai tikslai, pateikti tvarkingiau ir aiškiau:

Metiniai Tikslai

1. SKDV TRD projekto uždarymas:

  • 1.1. Užbaigti visus numatytus darbus (in scope).

2. Keliolapio kūrimas (Development):

  • 2.1. Parengti sprendimo dizainą (Solution design) ir patvirtinti jį su AT (Architektūros komanda).
  • 2.2. Vykdyti sprendimo kūrimą (Solution development) pagal suplanuotą grafiką.

3. Incidentų reagavimo laikas:

  • 3.1. Užtikrinti, kad incidentų reagavimo laikas neviršytų 4 valandų.

4. VE (Verslo Efektyvumo) sprendimų dokumentacija:

  • 4.1. Patvirtinti VE dokumentacijos standartą.
  • 4.2. Užtikrinti, kad visi kuriami sprendimai būtų dokumentuojami pagal patvirtintą standartą.

5. Savarankiškas UiPath VE ūkio palaikymas:

  • 5.1. Ugdyti verslo analitikų kompetencijas, kad jie galėtų savarankiškai palaikyti UiPath VE ūkį.
  • 5.2. Aktyviai bendradarbiauti su komandos nariais, deleguoti užduotis ir siekti sėkmingo projekto užbaigimo.

Papildomi patarimai:

  • Kiekvienam tikslui galite pridėti konkrečius terminus, pvz., "SKDV TRD projekto uždarymas: 2024 m. gruodžio 31 d.".
  • Suskirstykite didesnius tikslus į mažesnius, įgyvendinamus žingsnius.
  • Reguliariai peržiūrėkite savo tikslus ir atitinkamai koreguokite veiksmų planą.
  • Naudokite SMART tikslų formulavimo metodiką, kad tikslai būtų:
    • S (Specific) - konkretūs
    • M (Measurable) - išmatuojami
    • A (Achievable) - pasiekiami
    • R (Relevant) - aktualūs
    • T (Time-bound) - apibrėžti laiko atžvilgiu

Tikiuosi, kad šis patobulintas sąrašas padės jums sėkmingai pasiekti savo metinius tikslus!

title: Prompted An error occurred in the ‘’ query. Expression.Error: The name 'CurrentDateTime' wasn't recognized.  Make sure it's spelled correctly.

safeHtmlItem.html:
You're absolutely right! My apologies. When I refactored the code to create the function, I missed a crucial step: removing the CurrentDateTime variable.

The error "The name 'CurrentDateTime' wasn't recognized" occurs because the CurrentDateTime variable is no longer defined within the scope of the function.

Here's the corrected function code:

(givenDateTime as datetime) =>
let
    CurrentYear = DateTime.Year(givenDateTime),
    MarchLastSunday =
        let
            FirstOfMonth = #datetime(CurrentYear, 3, 1, 0, 0, 0),
            LastDayOfMonth = Date.Day(Date.EndOfMonth(Date.From(FirstOfMonth))),
            LastSunday = List.Last(List.Select({LastDayOfMonth-6..LastDayOfMonth}, each Date.DayOfWeek(#date(CurrentYear, 3, _)) = Day.Sunday)),
            Result = #datetime(CurrentYear, 3, LastSunday, 2, 0, 0)
        in
            Result,
    OctoberLastSunday =
        let
            FirstOfMonth = #datetime(CurrentYear, 10, 1, 0, 0, 0),
            LastDayOfMonth = Date.Day(Date.EndOfMonth(Date.From(FirstOfMonth))),
            LastSunday = List.Last(List.Select({LastDayOfMonth-6..LastDayOfMonth}, each Date.DayOfWeek(#date(CurrentYear, 10, _)) = Day.Sunday)),
            Result = #datetime(CurrentYear, 10, LastSunday, 3, 0, 0)
        in
            Result,
    IsSummerTime = givenDateTime >= MarchLastSunday and givenDateTime < OctoberLastSunday,
    TimeOffset = if IsSummerTime then 3 else 2
in
    TimeOffset

Key Correction:

  • The CurrentDateTime = DateTime.LocalNow() line has been completely removed. This ensures that the function only uses the givenDateTime parameter.

How to Fix the Error:

  1. Open Power Query Editor: Open the Power Query Editor in Power BI Desktop or Excel.
  2. Edit the Function Query: Select the query where you defined the GetTimeOffset function.
  3. Open Advanced Editor: Open the Advanced Editor for that query.
  4. Replace with Corrected Code: Replace the existing code with the corrected code provided above.
  5. Click "Done": Click "Done" to save the changes.

Now, the function will correctly calculate the time offset based on the givenDateTime parameter without relying on any undefined variables.

how to adjust this to create a function that returns offset value on given date

safeHtmlItem.html:
(givenDateTime as datetime) =>
let
    CurrentYear = DateTime.Year(givenDateTime),
    MarchLastSunday =
        let
            FirstOfMonth = #datetime(CurrentYear, 3, 1, 0, 0, 0),
            LastDayOfMonth = Date.Day(Date.EndOfMonth(Date.From(FirstOfMonth))),
            LastSunday = List.Last(List.Select({LastDayOfMonth-6..LastDayOfMonth}, each Date.DayOfWeek(#date(CurrentYear, 3, _)) = Day.Sunday)),
            Result = #datetime(CurrentYear, 3, LastSunday, 2, 0, 0)
        in
            Result,
    OctoberLastSunday =
        let
            FirstOfMonth = #datetime(CurrentYear, 10, 1, 0, 0, 0),
            LastDayOfMonth = Date.Day(Date.EndOfMonth(Date.From(FirstOfMonth))),
            LastSunday = List.Last(List.Select({LastDayOfMonth-6..LastDayOfMonth}, each Date.DayOfWeek(#date(CurrentYear, 10, _)) = Day.Sunday)),
            Result = #datetime(CurrentYear, 10, LastSunday, 3, 0, 0)
        in
            Result,
    IsSummerTime = givenDateTime >= MarchLastSunday and givenDateTime < OctoberLastSunday,
    TimeOffset = if IsSummerTime then 3 else 2
in
    TimeOffset

Explanation of Changes:

  1. Function Definition:

    • (givenDateTime as datetime) => creates a function that takes a datetime value as input and returns a value.
    • givenDateTime is the parameter name, and as datetime specifies its data type.

  2. Using givenDateTime:

    • CurrentDateTime is replaced with givenDateTime throughout the function. This ensures that the function calculates the offset based on the provided date and time, not the current system date and time.
    • givenDateTime is used in the IsSummerTime comparison.
    • DateTime.Year(givenDateTime) is used to extract the year of the given date.

How to Use the Function:

  1. Open Power Query Editor: Open the Power Query Editor in Power BI Desktop or Excel.
  2. Create a Blank Query: Create a new blank query.
  3. Open Advanced Editor: Open the Advanced Editor for the blank query.
  4. Paste the Function: Paste the code provided above into the Advanced Editor.
  5. Click "Done": Click "Done" to save the function.
  6. Rename the Function: Rename the query to something descriptive, like GetTimeOffset.

Calling the Function:

You can now call the GetTimeOffset function from other queries or custom columns:

let
    Source = Table.FromRecords({[MyDateTime = #datetime(2023, 7, 15, 10, 0, 0)], [MyDateTime = #datetime(2023, 12, 25, 12, 0, 0)]}),
    AddTimeOffset = Table.AddColumn(Source, "Offset", each GetTimeOffset([MyDateTime]))
in
    AddTimeOffset
  • In this example, GetTimeOffset([MyDateTime]) calls the function for each row in the "MyDateTime" column, and the result (the time offset) is added to a new column called "Offset".
  • You can also call the function directly in the formula bar of a query, for example, GetTimeOffset(#datetime(2024,01,01,10,0,0)).

title: Prompted = Table.AddColumn(#"Added Custom2", "Custom", each = TimeOffset{0}[OffHours])

safeHtmlItem.html:
You're very close! The issue is with the = sign directly after each. In Power Query's Table.AddColumn, the each keyword is used to define a function that will be applied to each row of the table. The function must return a value. In your case, you simply want to return the value from TimeOffset{0}[OffHours].

Here's the corrected code:

= Table.AddColumn(#"Added Custom2", "Custom", each TimeOffset{0}[OffHours])

Explanation of the Correction:

  • each TimeOffset{0}[OffHours]: This is now a valid Power Query expression.
    • each indicates that this expression will be evaluated for each row of the table.
    • TimeOffset{0}[OffHours] retrieves the value from the first row of the "TimeOffset" table, from the "OffHours" column, and returns it.

How to Implement:

  1. Open Power Query Editor: Open the Power Query Editor in Power BI Desktop or Excel.
  2. Select the Step: Select the step in your query where you want to add the custom column (presumably "Added Custom2").
  3. Edit the Formula Bar: In the formula bar, replace the existing formula with the corrected formula shown above.
  4. Press Enter: Press Enter to apply the change.

Important Note:

  • As mentioned before, this code assumes that your "TimeOffset" table has only one row. If it has multiple rows, you'll need to modify the code to select the correct row, depending on your logic.
  • If your table "TimeOffset" can be empty, then the query will generate an error. You should add an error handling strategy.

title: Prompted My table called TimeOffset and column called OffHours. How to use this value in other curry when creating custom column

safeHtmlItem.html:
let
    Source = // Your existing table here
    AddCustomColumn = Table.AddColumn(Source, "AdjustedTime", each 
        let
            TimeOffsetValue = TimeOffset{0}[OffHours], // Access the value from the TimeOffset table
            MyTime = [YourTimeColumn] // Replace [YourTimeColumn] with the actual time column in your Source table
        in
            DateTime.AddZone(MyTime, TimeOffsetValue, false) // Adjust the time
    )
in
    AddCustomColumn

Explanation and Steps:

  1. Replace // Your existing table here:

    • Replace this comment with the actual name of your existing table that you want to add the custom column to.

  2. TimeOffset{0}[OffHours]:

    • TimeOffset refers to your table named "TimeOffset."
    • {0} accesses the first (and only) row of the "TimeOffset" table.
    • [OffHours] retrieves the value from the "OffHours" column in that row.
    • This line effectively extracts the single value you need from your "TimeOffset" table.

  3. [YourTimeColumn]:

    • Replace [YourTimeColumn] with the actual name of the column in your Source table that contains the time values you want to adjust. For example, if your time column is called "EventTime," you would write [EventTime].

  4. DateTime.AddZone(MyTime, TimeOffsetValue, false):

    • This is the function that adds the time offset to your time values.
    • MyTime is the time value from your column.
    • TimeOffsetValue is the value you extracted from the "TimeOffset" table.
    • false indicates that you're adding a fixed offset, not a time zone offset.

How to Implement in Power Query:

  1. Open Power Query Editor: In Power BI Desktop or Excel, open the Power Query Editor.
  2. Select Your Table: Select the table to which you want to add the custom column.
  3. Add Custom Column:
    • Go to the "Add Column" tab in the ribbon.
    • Click "Custom Column."
  4. Enter the Formula:
    • In the "Custom column formula" box, paste the code provided above.
    • Crucially: Replace // Your existing table here and [YourTimeColumn] with the correct table and column names.
  5. Name Your Column:
    • Give your new column a descriptive name (e.g., "AdjustedEventTime").
  6. Click "OK": Click "OK" to add the custom column.

Important Considerations:

  • Single Row in TimeOffset: This code assumes that your "TimeOffset" table has only one row. If it has multiple rows, you'll need to modify the code to select the correct row.
  • Data Types: Make sure that the "OffHours" column in your "TimeOffset" table is a whole number (or a number that can be used as a time offset). Also make sure that the column that you are adjusting is a datetime value.
  • Error Handling: If your "TimeOffset" table might be empty, you should add error handling to prevent the query from failing.
  • Parameters: As discussed earlier, using a parameter is generally a better practice for performance and maintainability.

title: Prompted i have a query with 1 column and 1 row, TimeOffset how to use its value dynaiclty in over queries

safeHtmlItem.html:
You can use the value from a single-cell, single-column Power Query as a parameter or a variable in other queries within the same Power BI or Excel workbook. Here's how to do it:

1. Create Your TimeOffset Query (If You Haven't Already):

Make sure you have the query that calculates your TimeOffset value. If you've used the corrected code from our previous conversation, you should have a query that returns a single number (2 or 3). Let's assume you've named this query TimeOffsetQuery.

2. Turn the TimeOffset Query into a Parameter (Recommended):

  • Open Power Query Editor: In Power BI Desktop or Excel, open the Power Query Editor.
  • Find Your TimeOffsetQuery: Locate the TimeOffsetQuery in the Queries pane.
  • Right-Click and "Convert to Parameter": Right-click on the TimeOffsetQuery and select "Convert to Parameter."
  • Parameter Settings:
    • Name: Give the parameter a descriptive name, like TimeOffsetParameter.
    • Type: Make sure the type is set to "Whole Number."
    • Current Value: The value will be automatically populated from your query.
  • Click "OK": Click "OK" to save the parameter.

3. Use the Parameter in Other Queries:

Now, you can reference the TimeOffsetParameter in other queries within your workbook.

  • Example: Adding the Time Offset to a DateTime:

    let
    Source = Table.FromRecords({[MyDateTime = #datetime(2023, 10, 27, 10, 0, 0)]}),
    AdjustedDateTime = Table.AddColumn(Source, "AdjustedDateTime", each DateTime.AddZone([MyDateTime], TimeOffsetParameter, false))
in
    AdjustedDateTime
    • In this example, TimeOffsetParameter is directly used as the offset value in DateTime.AddZone().

  • Example: Filtering Based on Time Offset:

    let
    Source = Table.FromRecords({[Value = 1], [Value = 2], [Value = 3], [Value = 4]}),
    FilteredRows = Table.SelectRows(Source, each [Value] = TimeOffsetParameter)
in
    FilteredRows
    • In this example, the table is filtered to only include rows where the Value column matches the TimeOffsetParameter.

4. Using the Query Directly(less recommended):

While using a parameter is generally the best practice, you can technically reference the TimeOffsetQuery directly in other queries. However, this is less efficient and can lead to performance issues if the TimeOffsetQuery is complex.

  • How to Reference Directly:

    let
    Source = Table.FromRecords({[MyDateTime = #datetime(2023, 10, 27, 10, 0, 0)]}),
    TimeOffsetValue = TimeOffsetQuery{0}[TimeOffset],
    AdjustedDateTime = Table.AddColumn(Source, "AdjustedDateTime", each DateTime.AddZone([MyDateTime], TimeOffsetValue, false))
in
    AdjustedDateTime
    • TimeOffsetQuery{0}[TimeOffset] accesses the first (and only) row of the TimeOffsetQuery and retrieves the value from the TimeOffset column.
    • This method is less efficient because every time a query uses TimeOffsetQuery, that query will be evaluated, and that evaluation can be costly.

Why Parameters Are Better:

  • Performance: Parameters are evaluated once and their values are stored, making them much faster than re-evaluating a query every time it's referenced.
  • Maintainability: Parameters are easier to manage and update.
  • Clarity: Parameters make your code more readable and self-documenting.
  • Flexibility: Parameters can be used in various contexts, including filters, calculations, and conditional logic.

By converting your TimeOffsetQuery into a parameter, you'll have a clean, efficient, and maintainable way to use its value in other queries within your Power BI or Excel workbook.

title: Prompted Expression.Error: The import DateTime.Year matches no exports. Did you miss a module reference?

safeHtmlItem.html:
You've encountered a common issue in Power Query (M language) when dealing with date/time functions: the DateTime functions are context-sensitive. In simpler terms, you need to ensure you're working with a DateTime value, not just a Date value, when using functions like DateTime.Year().

Here's how to fix the error in your Power Query code:

The Problem:

The error "Expression.Error: The import DateTime.Year matches no exports. Did you miss a module reference?" arises because DateTime.LocalNow() returns a DateTime value, but when you do Date.EndOfMonth(#date(CurrentYear, 3, 1)), you generate a Date value. When you then try to extract the Day from that date and use it to build a DateTime with #datetime, you are mixing data types and causing the error.

The Solution:

The core fix is to consistently use DateTime values throughout your calculations. Here's the corrected code:

let
    CurrentDateTime = DateTime.LocalNow(),
    CurrentYear = DateTime.Year(CurrentDateTime),
    MarchLastSunday =
        let
            FirstOfMonth = #datetime(CurrentYear, 3, 1, 0, 0, 0),
            LastDayOfMonth = Date.Day(Date.EndOfMonth(Date.From(FirstOfMonth))),
            LastSunday = List.Last(List.Select({LastDayOfMonth-6..LastDayOfMonth}, each Date.DayOfWeek(#date(CurrentYear, 3, _)) = Day.Sunday)),
            Result = #datetime(CurrentYear, 3, LastSunday, 2, 0, 0)
        in
            Result,
    OctoberLastSunday =
        let
            FirstOfMonth = #datetime(CurrentYear, 10, 1, 0, 0, 0),
            LastDayOfMonth = Date.Day(Date.EndOfMonth(Date.From(FirstOfMonth))),
            LastSunday = List.Last(List.Select({LastDayOfMonth-6..LastDayOfMonth}, each Date.DayOfWeek(#date(CurrentYear, 10, _)) = Day.Sunday)),
            Result = #datetime(CurrentYear, 10, LastSunday, 3, 0, 0)
        in
            Result,
    IsSummerTime = CurrentDateTime >= MarchLastSunday and CurrentDateTime < OctoberLastSunday,
    TimeOffset = if IsSummerTime then 3 else 2
in
    TimeOffset

Key Changes:

  1. Consistent DateTime Usage:

    • FirstOfMonth is now defined as #datetime(CurrentYear, 3, 1, 0, 0, 0) and #datetime(CurrentYear,10,1,0,0,0) ensuring that we are working with datetime values from the very start of the calculations.
    • Date.From(FirstOfMonth) converts the datetime to a date so that Date.EndOfMonth can function.
    • Date.Day(Date.EndOfMonth(Date.From(FirstOfMonth))) extracts the day as a number.

  2. Clarity and Readability:

    • The code maintains a structured approach with nested let expressions.
    • Added the variable FirstOfMonth to improve readability.
    • Added the variable Result to the nested let expressions to make the returned value more obvious.

How to Apply the Fix:

  1. Open Power Query Editor: In Power BI Desktop or Excel, open the Power Query Editor.
  2. Edit Your Query: Open the Advanced Editor for your existing query.
  3. Replace with Corrected Code: Replace the original code with the corrected code provided above.
  4. Click "Done": Click "Done" to apply the changes.

By making these changes, you'll resolve the "DateTime.Year matches no exports" error and ensure that your Power Query code correctly calculates the time offset based on daylight saving time.

title:
Prompted let
    CurrentDateTime = DateTime.LocalNow(),
    CurrentYear = DateTime.Year(CurrentDateTime),
    SummerTimeStart = #datetime(CurrentYear, 3, Date.EndOfMonth(#date(CurrentYear, 3, 1))[Day], 2, 0, 0),
    SummerTimeEnd = #datetime(CurrentYear, 10, Date.EndOfMonth(#date(CurrentYear, 10, 1))[Day], 3, 0, 0),
    IsSummerTime = CurrentDateTime >= SummerTimeStart and CurrentDateTime < SummerTimeEnd,

fix this powerqury m

safeHtmlItem.html:
let
    CurrentDateTime = DateTime.LocalNow(),
    CurrentYear = DateTime.Year(CurrentDateTime),
    MarchLastSunday =
        let
            LastDay = Date.EndOfMonth(#date(CurrentYear, 3, 1))[Day],
            LastSunday = List.Last(List.Select({LastDay-6..LastDay}, each Date.DayOfWeek(#date(CurrentYear, 3, _)) = Day.Sunday))
        in
            #datetime(CurrentYear, 3, LastSunday, 2, 0, 0),
    OctoberLastSunday =
        let
            LastDay = Date.EndOfMonth(#date(CurrentYear, 10, 1))[Day],
            LastSunday = List.Last(List.Select({LastDay-6..LastDay}, each Date.DayOfWeek(#date(CurrentYear, 10, _)) = Day.Sunday))
        in
            #datetime(CurrentYear, 10, LastSunday, 3, 0, 0),
    IsSummerTime = CurrentDateTime >= MarchLastSunday and CurrentDateTime < OctoberLastSunday,
    TimeOffset = if IsSummerTime then 3 else 2
in
    TimeOffset

Explanation of Changes and Improvements:

  1. Accurate Calculation of Last Sunday:

    • The original code attempted to find the last day of the month and then assumed it was the last Sunday. This is incorrect. The date of the switch to summer time is always the last sunday of march, and the date of the switch back is the last sunday of october.
    • The corrected code now uses a more robust method:
      • It gets the last day of the month (March or October).
      • It creates a list of the last seven days of the month.
      • It filters this list to find the day that is a Sunday.
      • It then takes the last Sunday from the filtered list.
    • This ensures that the correct date for the start and end of daylight saving time is calculated.

  2. Clarity and Readability:

    • The code is now more organized with nested let expressions to clearly define the calculations for the last Sunday of March and October.
    • The variable names are more descriptive (MarchLastSunday, OctoberLastSunday).

Why the Original Code Was Incorrect:

  • The original code's Date.EndOfMonth approach only provided the last day of the month, not necessarily the last Sunday.
  • Daylight saving time transitions are based on the last Sunday of those months, not just the last day.

How to Use This Corrected Code:

  1. Open Power Query Editor: In Power BI Desktop or Excel, open the Power Query Editor.
  2. Create a Blank Query: Create a new blank query.
  3. Open Advanced Editor: Open the Advanced Editor for the query.
  4. Paste the Code: Paste the corrected code into the Advanced Editor.
  5. Click "Done": Click "Done" to apply the changes.
  6. The result will be the time offset (2 or 3) based on the current date and whether daylight saving time is in effect.

title: Prompted Write a discription for supervisors security role, that role will be able to see other people tasks

safeHtmlItem.html:
Supervisor Security Role Description

Role: Supervisor

Security Permissions:

  • Task Visibility:
    • View tasks assigned to all team members within their direct reporting hierarchy.
    • Access task details, including status, due dates, and progress.
    • View comments and attachments related to tasks.
  • Task Management:
    • Assign tasks to team members within their direct reporting hierarchy.
    • Reassign tasks within their team.
    • Set deadlines and priorities for tasks.
    • Provide feedback and comments on tasks.
  • Report Generation:
    • Generate reports on team performance, task completion rates, and overall project progress.
    • Access historical data and trends to identify areas for improvement.

Note:

  • Supervisors should be granted the minimum necessary permissions to perform their duties effectively.
  • Regular security reviews and audits should be conducted to ensure proper access controls and data protection.
  • Consider implementing role-based access control (RBAC) to further refine permissions and minimize security risks.

By carefully defining and managing supervisor security roles, organizations can balance the need for visibility and control with data privacy and security best practices.

title: Prompted DateTimeZone.FixedLocalNow + #duration(0, 2, 0, 0) in m query dont work

safeHtmlItem.html:
Understanding the Issue with DateTimeZone.FixedLocalNow + #duration(0, 2, 0, 0)

While DateTimeZone.FixedLocalNow provides a fixed local datetime with timezone information, adding a duration directly to it might not always yield the expected result. This is because time zones can have complex rules, including daylight saving time (DST) transitions, which can affect how duration additions are interpreted.

A More Reliable Approach: Using DateTimeZone.SwitchZone

To accurately add or subtract hours to a datetime while considering time zone information, it's recommended to use the DateTimeZone.SwitchZone function. This function allows you to change the timezone of a datetime value, effectively adjusting it for different time zones.

Here's how you can use it to add 2 hours to the current local time:

let
    LocalTimePlus2Hours = DateTimeZone.SwitchZone(DateTimeZone.FixedLocalNow(), 2)
in
    LocalTimePlus2Hours

Explanation:

  1. DateTimeZone.FixedLocalNow(): Gets the current local datetime with timezone information.
  2. DateTimeZone.SwitchZone(): Takes the local datetime as input and switches its timezone by adding 2 hours. This operation effectively adjusts the datetime to a new timezone that is 2 hours ahead.

Additional Considerations:

  • DST Transitions: If your operation spans a DST transition, ensure that you handle the potential hour difference correctly. You might need to adjust the duration added or subtracted based on the specific DST rules.
  • Time Zone Specifics: Be aware of the specific time zone rules for your region, as they can vary.
  • Power BI Service Display: Remember that Power BI Service often displays data in UTC. To visualize the correct local time in your reports, you might need to apply time zone conversions within the Power BI model or use custom visuals that can handle time zone display.

By using DateTimeZone.SwitchZone, you can reliably add or subtract hours to a local datetime while considering time zone complexities.

title: Prompted in pbi i use DateTime.LocalNow() but i still got utc time i need to get my local time

safeHtmlItem.html:
Understanding the DateTime.LocalNow() Function in Power BI

While DateTime.LocalNow() is designed to return the current local time, it's important to note that Power BI's rendering and data storage often involve UTC time. This can sometimes lead to discrepancies between the local time you expect and the displayed time.

Here are some potential solutions to ensure accurate local time display in your Power BI reports:

1. Client-Side Timezone Conversion:

  • Power BI Desktop:

    • Use DAX to calculate the difference between UTC and your local timezone.
    • Add this difference to the UTC timestamp to get the local time.
    • Display the calculated local time in your visuals.

  • Power BI Service:

    • While Power BI Service primarily uses UTC, you can potentially use browser settings or user preferences to influence time zone display.
    • However, this might not be entirely reliable and can vary across different browsers and devices.

2. Server-Side Timezone Configuration:

  • Power BI Service:
    • If you have control over the server where Power BI Service is hosted, you can configure the server's timezone to match your local time. This might require coordination with your IT team.
    • However, this approach might affect other users who access the report from different time zones.

3. Custom Visuals:

  • Consider using custom visuals that allow for more granular control over time zone handling.
  • These custom visuals can often be configured to display data in the desired time zone, providing more flexibility.

Specific DAX Example:

LocalTime = 
    VAR UTCNow = DateTime.LocalNow()
    VAR TimeZoneOffset = -5 // Adjust this to your specific timezone offset
    RETURN
        UTCNow + #duration(0, TimeZoneOffset, 0, 0)

Additional Tips:

  • Data Source Timezone:
    • Ensure that your data source is providing timestamps in the correct time zone.
    • If necessary, adjust the time zone within your data source or during data import.
  • Power BI Desktop Settings:
    • Check your Power BI Desktop settings to verify that the correct time zone is selected.
  • Data Model Time Zone:
    • If you're working with a complex data model, ensure that the time zone is consistent across different tables and measures.

By combining these techniques and considering the specific requirements of your Power BI report, you can accurately display local time and avoid any timezone-related issues.

perrasyk tvarkingai

safeHtmlItem.html:
Supratimas ir tikslų išgryninimas

Problema:

  • Kai Power BI ataskaitos atnaujinimas nepavyksta, vartotojas gauna pranešimą, tačiau ataskaita nėra atnaujinama automatiškai.
  • Vartotojas turi kreiptis į atsakingą asmenį, kad šis rankiniu būdu paleistų atnaujinimą.
  • Dėl to prarandamas laikas, mažėja darbo efektyvumas ir didėja apkrova atsakingam asmeniui.

Sprendimas:

Sukuriame automatizuotą procesą (flow), kuris:

  1. Stebės gaunamus el. laiškus apie nepavykusį Power BI ataskaitos atnaujinimą.
  2. Automatiškai bandys iš naujo atnaujinti ataskaitą.
  3. Informuos atsakingą asmenį tik tuo atveju, jei pakartotinis bandymas taip pat nepavyksta.

Tikslai:

  • Pagerinti vartotojo patirtį: Ataskaitos bus atnaujinamos automatiškai, nebereikės laukti pagalbos.
  • Sumažinti darbo krūvį: Atsakingam asmeniui nebereikės rankiniu būdu paleisti atnaujinimų, išskyrus išskirtines situacijas.
  • Padidinti sistemos patikimumą: Problemos bus sprendžiamos greičiau, o vartotojai turės prieigą prie aktualiausių duomenų.

Siūlomas sprendimas: Power Automate flow

1. Sukurti Power Automate flow:

  • Trigers: Naujas el. laiškas, kuriame yra specifinis raktažodis (pvz., "Power BI ataskaitos atnaujinimo klaida").
  • Veiksmai:
    • Gauti el. laiško detales: Išskirti ataskaitos pavadinimą arba ID iš el. laiško turinio.
    • Atnaujinti Power BI ataskaitą: Naudoti Power BI Connector, kad būtų galima sąveikauti su Power BI paslauga ir inicijuoti ataskaitos atnaujinimą.
    • Sąlyga: Jei ataskaitos atnaujinimas buvo sėkmingas, baigti flow.
    • Siųsti el. laišką: Jei ataskaitos atnaujinimas nepavyko, išsiųsti pranešimą atsakingam asmeniui, nurodant ataskaitos pavadinimą ir klaidos detales.

2. Konfigūruoti Power BI duomenų šaltinį:

  • Įsitikinkite, kad Power BI duomenų šaltinis yra tinkamai sukonfigūruotas ir turi reikiamus leidimus.
  • Patikrinkite, ar nėra jokių ryšio problemų arba duomenų šaltinio pasiekiamumo klaidų.

3. Apmokyti vartotojus:

  • Informuoti vartotojus apie naują automatizuotą procesą.
  • Paaiškinti, kad gavus pranešimą apie nepavykusį atnaujinimą, nereikia imtis jokių veiksmų, nes sistema bandys išspręsti problemą automatiškai.

Papildomos rekomendacijos

  • Klaidų žurnalas: Įdiegti mechanizmą, kuris kauptų informaciją apie nepavykusius atnaujinimus. Tai padės identifikuoti pasikartojančias problemas ir išanalizuoti jų priežastis.
  • Išsamios klaidos žinutės: Užtikrinti, kad el. laiškuose siunčiamos išsamios klaidos žinutės, kurios padėtų greičiau diagnozuoti problemą.
  • Testuoti: Kruopščiai išbandyti flow įvairiose situacijose, kad įsitikintumėte, jog jis veikia kaip tikėtasi.
  • Apsvarstyti alternatyvias platformas: Be Power Automate, galima naudoti ir kitas platformas, tokias kaip Azure Logic Apps ar IFTTT, kurios taip pat leidžia kurti automatizuotus procesus.

Išsamus techninis sprendimas priklausys nuo jūsų konkretaus Power BI aplinkos konfigūravimo ir naudojamų įrankių.

Ar norėtumėte gauti daugiau informacijos apie konkrečius veiksmus, kuriuos reikia atlikti, kad įgyvendintumėte šį sprendimą?

Jei taip, prašau pateikti daugiau informacijos apie:

  • Jūsų naudojamą Power BI versiją
  • El. pašto sistemą

Aš mielai padėsiu jums sukurti išsamų ir efektyvų sprendimą.

Įsivaizduokite, kad turite robotą, kuris nuolat tikrina jūsų el. paštą. Šis robotas yra sukurtas naudojant programą, vadinamą Power Automate.

Ką šis robotas daro:

  • Saugus namas: Visas šis procesas yra saugus, nes naudojamas specialus saugumo tarnybos serveris, vadinamas Azure AD. Jis tarsi saugus namas, kuriame saugomi visi raktai ir leidimai.

Trumpai tariant: Šis robotas padeda automatiškai išspręsti problemas su jūsų duomenimis, kad jums nereiktų visko daryti rankomis. Jis veikia greitai, patikimai ir saugiai.

title: Prompted remake it as case, appearantly my values is not null but blank

safeHtmlItem.html:
Understanding the Problem:

It seems you have a scenario where values might appear blank, even though they're not technically null. This could be due to whitespace characters or other non-visible characters. To accurately handle this in a CASE statement, we need to consider these blank values as a specific condition.

Here's a revised CASE statement to handle blank values:

CASE 
    WHEN TRIM([TransportOrderOrderReference]) <> '' THEN TRIM([TransportOrderOrderReference])
    WHEN TRIM([TransportOrderNumber]) <> '' THEN TRIM([TransportOrderNumber])
    ELSE NULL
END AS WayBillID

Explanation:

  1. TRIM function: This function removes leading and trailing whitespace characters from the specified string.
  2. CASE statement:
    • First condition: Checks if TransportOrderOrderReference, after trimming, is not an empty string. If it's not, it returns the trimmed value as the WayBillID.
    • Second condition: If the first condition fails, it checks if TransportOrderNumber, after trimming, is not an empty string. If it's not, it returns the trimmed value as the WayBillID.
    • ELSE condition: If both conditions fail, it returns NULL.

Additional Considerations:

  • Specific Whitespace Characters: If you're dealing with specific whitespace characters (like tabs or newlines) that TRIM might not handle, you can use more specific string manipulation functions to remove them.
  • Data Quality: It's also worth considering the underlying data quality and why these values might appear blank. Addressing the root cause of the issue might be more effective in the long run.

By using this CASE statement with the TRIM function, you can effectively handle blank values and ensure accurate WayBillID assignments.

The SQL statement you provided:

Coalesce([TransportOrderOrderReference], [TransportOrderNumber]) As WayBillID

is syntactically correct and uses the COALESCE function effectively. This function returns the first non-null value from the list of expressions provided.

Here's how it works:

  1. Checks TransportOrderOrderReference: If this value is not null, it's returned as the WayBillID.
  2. Checks TransportOrderNumber: If TransportOrderOrderReference is null, but TransportOrderNumber is not, the latter is returned as the WayBillID.
  3. Returns NULL: If both values are null, the COALESCE function returns null.

Therefore, this statement is designed to:

  • Prioritize the TransportOrderOrderReference as the WayBillID if available.
  • If not, it falls back to the TransportOrderNumber.
  • If both are null, it assigns a null value to WayBillID.

Is there a specific issue or question you have about this statement? Please feel free to provide more context or ask any specific questions you may have.

Here are some potential scenarios where you might use this statement:

  • Report Generation: To create a consistent WayBillID for different data sources.
  • Data Integration: To combine data from multiple sources with different identifiers.

title: Prompted what is the main porpose of sql view, what is the best practice when to create view or when to query indepedent complicated queries

safeHtmlItem.html:
SQL Views: A Virtual Table

An SQL view is a virtual table based on the result-set of an SQL statement. It doesn't store data directly, but rather provides a different way to look at the data stored in underlying tables.

Main Purpose

SQL views are primarily used to:

  1. Simplify Complex Queries:

    • Abstraction: Hide complex SQL logic from users, making it easier to query data.
    • Reusability: Create reusable, named queries for frequent operations.

  2. Enhance Data Security:

    • Restrict Access: Limit user access to specific data subsets.
    • Data Masking: Mask sensitive data while providing necessary information.

  3. Improve Data Consistency:

    • Centralized Logic: Enforce data consistency by defining complex calculations and transformations in one place.

  4. Optimize Performance:

    • Materialized Views: Store pre-calculated results, improving query performance for frequently accessed data.

Best Practices for Creating Views

  • Simplicity: Keep view definitions clear and concise.
  • Performance: Consider indexing underlying tables and using materialized views for performance-critical queries.
  • Security: Grant appropriate permissions to views to control data access.
  • Maintainability: Regularly review and update views to reflect changes in the underlying data.
  • Naming Conventions: Use clear and consistent naming conventions for views.

When to Create a View vs. Independent Queries

Create a View When:

  • Frequent Reuse: The query is used multiple times in different contexts.
  • Complex Logic: The query involves complex joins, aggregations, or calculations.
  • Data Security: You need to restrict access to specific data subsets.
  • Performance Optimization: Materialized views can significantly improve query performance.

Use Independent Queries When:

  • One-Time Queries: The query is used only once or infrequently.
  • Simple Queries: The query is straightforward and doesn't involve complex logic.
  • Dynamic Data: The data requirements change frequently, making a view less suitable.

In Conclusion

SQL views are a powerful tool for managing and accessing data in a database. By understanding their purpose and best practices, you can effectively leverage them to simplify complex queries, enhance data security, and improve overall database performance.

title:
Prompted AverageMaterialRss = CALCULATE(AVERAGE(dimAssignTaskFactHours[KronosHours]), ALLEXCEPT(dimAssignTaskFactHours[KronosHours],ps_ra_materialtype))

fix

safeHtmlItem.html:
The DAX measure AverageMaterialRss you provided seems to have a potential issue in its ALLEXCEPT function. The ALLEXCEPT function is designed to remove all filters from a table except for the specified columns. However, in your case, you're only specifying one column: ps_ra_materialtype.

If you want to calculate the average KronosHours for each ps_ra_materialtype, while still respecting other filters (like date, region, etc.), you should remove all filters except for the ps_ra_materialtype column.

Here's the corrected DAX measure:

AverageMaterialRss = 
CALCULATE(
    AVERAGE(dimAssignTaskFactHours[KronosHours]),
    ALLEXCEPT(dimAssignTaskFactHours, dimAssignTaskFactHours[ps_ra_materialtype])
)

This modified measure will calculate the average KronosHours for each ps_ra_materialtype, while still respecting other filters applied to the table.

Additional Considerations:

  • Context Transition: If you're using this measure in a matrix or table visual, ensure that the ps_ra_materialtype column is included in the rows or columns of the visual. This will allow the measure to calculate the average for each material type.
  • Filter Context: Be mindful of the filter context in your visual. If you have other filters applied (e.g., date range, region), the ALLEXCEPT function will ensure that the average is calculated within the context of those filters.
  • Data Model Relationships: Verify that the relationships between your tables are correct, especially the relationship between dimAssignTaskFactHours and the dimension table containing ps_ra_materialtype.

By addressing these points and using the corrected DAX measure, you should be able to accurately calculate the average KronosHours for each ps_ra_materialtype in your Power BI report.

title: Prompted filtered is dynamic via slicer

safeHtmlItem.html:
The DAX measure we provided already accounts for dynamic filtering based on slicers. The ALLEXCEPT function ensures that only the filter on the UnitType column is removed, while other filters, such as those applied by slicers, remain in effect.

Here's a breakdown of how it works:

  1. Slicer Selection: When you select a specific unit type from the slicer, it applies a filter to the UnitType column in your data model.
  2. DAX Measure Calculation: The CALCULATE function with ALLEXCEPT is triggered.
  3. ALLEXCEPT Function: Removes all filters except for the UnitType filter. This means that the average is calculated only for the selected unit type, taking into account any other filters applied (e.g., date range, region, etc.).

To ensure the measure works correctly:

  1. Verify the Relationship: Make sure there's a correct relationship between your fact table (containing the UnitCount and UnitType columns) and any dimension tables used in your slicers.
  2. Check Slicer Configuration: Ensure that your slicer is correctly configured to filter the appropriate column (UnitType in this case).
  3. Test the Measure: Try selecting different unit types from the slicer and observe how the average line adjusts accordingly.

title: Prompted but i need to calcualte for the filtered unit type only

safeHtmlItem.html:
Calculating a Filtered Average Based on Unit Type

Understanding the Requirement:

You want to calculate the average unit count for a specific unit type, even when the report is filtered by other criteria (like date). This means the average should adjust dynamically based on the selected unit type.

DAX Measure for Filtered Average:

Here's a DAX measure to achieve this:

FilteredAverage =
CALCULATE(
    AVERAGE(YourTableName[UnitCount]),
    ALLEXCEPT(YourTableName, YourTableName[UnitType])
)

Explanation:

  1. CALCULATE(): This function allows you to modify the filter context of a measure.
  2. AVERAGE(YourTableName[UnitCount]): Calculates the average unit count.
  3. ALLEXCEPT(YourTableName, YourTableName[UnitType]): Removes all filters except for the UnitType column. This ensures the average is calculated based on the currently selected unit type, regardless of other filters.

Adding the Filtered Average Line to the Visual:

  1. Create a New Measure: Paste the DAX formula above into a new measure.
  2. Add the Measure to the Visual: Drag this new measure to the Y-axis of your line chart.
  3. Format the Line: Customize the appearance of the line (color, style, etc.) to distinguish it from the other lines.

Additional Considerations:

  • Multiple Unit Types: If you have multiple unit types, you might want to use a slicer or a filter to select the desired unit type. The filtered average will automatically adjust based on the selection.
  • Time Intelligence Functions: If you need to calculate a rolling average for a specific unit type, you can combine the CALCULATE and ALLEXCEPT functions with time intelligence functions like DATEADD and DATESBETWEEN.
  • Conditional Formatting: Highlight periods where the unit count is significantly above or below the filtered average using conditional formatting.

By following these steps and understanding the ALLEXCEPT function, you can create dynamic average lines that adapt to different filter contexts, providing valuable insights into your data.

title: Prompted  i did this average but it still adjusts to time filter

safeHtmlItem.html:
Understanding the Issue: Dynamic vs. Static Average Line

If your average line is adjusting to time filters, it means it's calculating the average based on the filtered data. To create a static average line that remains constant regardless of the time filter, you'll need to use a measure that ignores the filter context.

Solution: Using the ALL() Function

The ALL() function removes all filters from a specific column or table. In this case, we want to remove the filter on the date column to calculate the overall average.

Here's the DAX measure to calculate the static average:

Static Average = CALCULATE(AVERAGE(YourTableName[UnitCount]), ALL(YourTableName[Date]))

How it Works:

  1. CALCULATE(): This function allows you to modify the filter context of a measure.
  2. AVERAGE(YourTableName[UnitCount]): Calculates the average unit count.
  3. ALL(YourTableName[Date]): Removes any filter on the Date column, ensuring the average is calculated across the entire dataset.

Adding the Static Average Line to the Visual:

  1. Create a New Measure: Paste the DAX formula above into a new measure.
  2. Add the Measure to the Visual: Drag this new measure to the Y-axis of your line chart.
  3. Format the Line: Customize the appearance of the line (color, style, etc.) to distinguish it from the dynamic line.

Additional Tips:

  • Time Intelligence Functions: If you want to calculate a rolling average over a specific period, consider using time intelligence functions like DATEADD and DATESBETWEEN.
  • Conditional Formatting: Highlight periods where the unit count is significantly above or below the static average using conditional formatting.
  • Visual Level Filters: If you need to filter the data without affecting the static average, use visual level filters.

By following these steps, you should be able to create a static average line that provides a fixed reference point for comparison, regardless of the applied time filters.

If you still encounter issues, please provide more specific details about your data model, DAX measures, and the exact behavior you're observing.

title:
Prompted AverageHoursPerUnitKronos = 
DIVIDE(
    SUM(dimAssignTaskFactHours[KronosHours]),
    SUM(ps_ra_pvd_assigntasks[UnitCount]),
    BLANK()
)

mdifiy to take unit count only for those rows where kronoshours is not blank

safeHtmlItem.html:
AverageHoursPerUnitKronos = 
DIVIDE(
  SUM(dimAssignTaskFactHours[KronosHours]),
  SUMX(
    FILTER(
      ALL(ps_ra_pvd_assigntasks),
      NOT(ISBLANK(RELATED(dimAssignTaskFactHours[KronosHours])))
    ),
    ps_ra_pvd_assigntasks[UnitCount]
  ),
  BLANK()
)

Explanation:

  1. Outer DIVIDE Function:

    • Remains the same as before, dividing the total Kronos hours by the adjusted unit count.

  2. Inner SUMX Function:

    • Iterates over a filtered table of ps_ra_pvd_assigntasks.
    • FILTER Function: Filters the table to include only rows where the related KronosHours is not blank. This ensures that only relevant unit counts are considered.
    • SUM Function: Sums the UnitCount column of the filtered table, giving the adjusted unit count.

In essence:

  • The formula first filters the ps_ra_pvd_assigntasks table to include only rows associated with non-blank KronosHours.
  • Then, it calculates the total unit count from this filtered table.
  • Finally, it divides the total Kronos hours by this adjusted unit count.

This modification ensures that the average hours per unit calculation is based on accurate unit counts, considering only those units associated with actual Kronos hours.

title:

)

safeHtmlItem.html:
Understanding the DAX Formula:

The provided DAX formula calculates the Average Hours Per Unit (Kronos). Let's break down the components:

1. DIVIDE Function:

  • Purpose: Performs division, handling potential division-by-zero errors gracefully.
  • Arguments:
    • Numerator: SUM(dimAssignTaskFactHours[KronosHours])
    • Denominator: SUM(ps_ra_pvd_assigntasks[UnitCount])
    • Alternate Result: BLANK() (used when the denominator is zero)

2. Numerator: SUM(dimAssignTaskFactHours[KronosHours])

  • Purpose: Calculates the total Kronos hours by summing the KronosHours column from the dimAssignTaskFactHours table.

3. Denominator: SUM(ps_ra_pvd_assigntasks[UnitCount])

  • Purpose: Calculates the total number of units by summing the UnitCount column from the ps_ra_pvd_assigntasks table.

Overall Calculation:

The formula divides the total Kronos hours by the total number of units
